content.plain_english: This week in Year 5 maths, your child is working on Decimals. The learning focuses on: solve addition and subtraction multi-step problems in contexts, deciding which operations and methods to use and why; use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling; and measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children begin by folding paper, using fraction bars, and drawing bar models to see the concept visually before working with numbers and symbols. Discussion in pairs and small groups helps them explain and justify their reasoning using precise mathematical vocabulary.
content.home_activity: Look at price labels in the kitchen — tins, packets, or bottles. Practise reading decimal amounts aloud and round prices to the nearest pound. Estimate the total cost of a few items — this puts decimals in a real, meaningful context.
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to solve addition and subtraction multi-step problems in contexts, deciding which operations and methods to use and why?
Was it easy or tricky to use all four operations to solve problems involving measure, for example length, mass, volume and money, using decimal notation including scaling? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: The dot separating the whole number part from the fractional part — e.g. 3.7.
The first digit after the decimal point — each tenth is one part out of ten.
The second digit after the decimal point — one part out of a hundred.
Having the same value — e.g. 0.5 = ½ = 50%.
Give a decimal to a certain number of decimal places.

example_questions: Convert 0.35 to a fraction in its simplest form.
Write ⅜ as a decimal and as a percentage.
content.times_table_tip: By Year 5 children should know all their times tables with speed and confidence. Ask quick-fire questions at dinner — mixing tables so they recall facts rather than just reciting sequences. Can they answer in under 3 seconds?
